---
title: "Exercises"
subtitle: "A nine-section problem set on a second dyadic dataset"
---

These exercises ask you to apply every method in the [tutorials](../../tutorials/indistinguishable/index.html) to a *new* dyadic dataset. The dataset has three outcomes, three predictors, and three dyad-level moderators, so each analytic step has to be repeated three times — once per outcome. That repetition is the point: in real research, you fit the same model several times and interpret each fit in light of the data-generating process.

::: {.callout-important}
## Before you start
- The exercise dataset is [`data/exercise_data.RData`](../../data/exercise-data.html). It is *different* from the tutorial dataset. Read its [documentation](../../data/exercise-data.html) first.
- Each section has a collapsible **Hints & solution outline** block at the bottom. Open it only after you have produced the model and your one-sentence interpretation.
- The reflection prompts are the most important part. The numerical answers are reproducible from the DGP; your *interpretation* is what the exercise is testing.
:::

## The nine sections

::: {.card-grid}

::: {.card}
### [Section 1 — Data simulation & inspection]{.card-title-link}

Orient yourself to the exercise dataset before any modelling.
Compute the ICC for each outcome, summarise the moderators, and
identify which variables show gender mean differences.

<span class="tag tag-beginner">Beginner</span>

[Open section →](section-1.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 2 — Indistinguishable dyads: MLM]{.card-title-link}

Fit the full Actor–Partner Interdependence Model in long format
for each of the three outcomes, compare with a partner-free
baseline, and write a two-sentence interpretation.

<span class="tag tag-mlm">MLM</span>
<span class="tag tag-intermediate">Intermediate</span>

[Open section →](section-2.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 3 — Indistinguishable dyads: SEM]{.card-title-link}

Three SEM paradigms for indistinguishable dyads: cluster-robust
SE, two-level within/between decomposition, and the wide-format
equality-constrained model. The formal indistinguishability test.

<span class="tag tag-sem">SEM</span>
<span class="tag tag-advanced">Advanced</span>

[Open section →](section-3.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 4 — Distinguishable dyads: MLM moderator]{.card-title-link}

Add gender as a moderator of actor and partner effects. Use
simple slopes to extract the conditional effects for each gender.

<span class="tag tag-mlm-moderator">MLM + gender</span>
<span class="tag tag-intermediate">Intermediate</span>

[Open section →](section-4.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 5 — Distinguishable dyads: SEM (moderation)]{.card-title-link}

Two-level SEM with gender × predictor interactions at the
within-dyad level. Calculated simple slopes in `lavaan`.

<span class="tag tag-sem-moderation">SEM + gender</span>
<span class="tag tag-advanced">Advanced</span>

[Open section →](section-5.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 6 — Distinguishable dyads: SEM wide]{.card-title-link}

The unconstrained / slopes-equal / fully-constrained model
sequence and the three nested likelihood ratio tests.

<span class="tag tag-sem-wide">SEM wide</span>
<span class="tag tag-advanced">Advanced</span>

[Open section →](section-6.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 7 — Two-intercept models]{.card-title-link}

The `0 + gender` parameterisation trick. Three nested MLMs and
their LRTs. The SEM analogue.

<span class="tag tag-mlm-two-intercept">Two-intercept</span>
<span class="tag tag-intermediate">Intermediate</span>

[Open section →](section-7.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 8 — Moderated APIM (the centrepiece)]{.card-title-link}

The focal crossover-by-moderator interaction for each outcome,
in both MLM and SEM. Simple slopes at low and high levels of
the moderator.

<span class="tag tag-mlm-moderated">MLM + SEM</span>
<span class="tag tag-advanced">Advanced</span>

[Open section →](section-8.html)
:::

::: {.card}
### [Section 9 — Integrative exercise]{.card-title-link}

A kitchen-sink multilevel model and the matching SEM. One
outcome. Three moderator levels. A 4–6 sentence interpretation
paragraph.

<span class="tag tag-advanced">Advanced</span>

[Open section →](section-9.html)
:::

:::

## Dataset cheat sheet

| Element | Value |
|---|---|
| N couples | 250 (500 individuals) |
| Outcomes | `engagement`, `performance`, `creativity` |
| Predictors | `affect`, `sdt`, `job_crafting` |
| Moderators | `live_together` (binary), `years_together` (continuous), `time_spent_this_morning_together` (continuous) |
| Distinguishing variable | `gender` (`male` / `female`) |
| Long format | `ddl2` |
| Wide format | `ddw2` |

## Suggested order

Sections 1–8 follow the same order as the [tutorials](../../tutorials/indistinguishable/index.html). Section 9 is the integrative capstone — do it last.

If you are short on time, the minimum useful set is **1, 2, 4, 8**: data inspection, indistinguishable MLM, gender moderator, and the moderated APIM. These four cover roughly 80% of what you will need in your own research.
